shortDescription.text: Internal API relies on static shared secret with no network-layer enforcement
Tenant ID supplied by caller is trusted without ownership verification
No rate limiting or quota enforcement on internal API endpoints
Mail endpoint allows arbitrary recipient, subject, and body with no sender verification or rate limiting
Raw exception messages returned to caller may leak internal details
Workspace endpoint assigns owner by email without verifying caller-owner relationship

message.text: The plugin_inner_api_only decorator authenticates requests using a static shared key (INNER_API_KEY_FOR_PLUGIN) compared via simple string equality from the X-Inner-Api-Key header. There is no network-layer restriction (IP allowlist, mTLS, etc.) enforced in the application. If this API is accidentally exposed beyond the internal network — through misconfigured reverse proxy, cloud load balancer, or container networking — any attacker with the key (or ability to brute-force it) gains full access to invoke arbitrary LLM models, tools, apps, encryption/decryption, and file upload signing on any tenant.

The get_user_tenant decorator in plugin/wraps.py extracts tenant_id and user_id directly from the JSON request body and uses them to look up the tenant and user without verifying that the authenticated caller (plugin daemon) is authorized to act on behalf of that specific tenant. A compromised or malicious plugin can specify any tenant_id to invoke LLM models, tools, or apps belonging to an arbitrary tenant. The code comment explicitly acknowledges: 'user_id is not trusted, it could be maliciously set to any value'. If user_id does not exist, a new EndUser is silently created for the target tenant.

The get_user function auto-creates an EndUser record for any tenant_id + user_id pair when the user is not found. A caller can enumerate or pollute tenants by specifying arbitrary tenant_id values, creating phantom user entries in tenants they should not have access to.

All plugin invoke endpoints (LLM, tool, rerank, TTS, speech2text, moderation, app, summary, text-embedding, parameter-extractor, question-classifier) have no rate limiting, request size limits, or quota enforcement at the API layer. A compromised plugin daemon key allows unlimited invocations of expensive LLM operations across all tenants, enabling resource exhaustion and cost amplification attacks.

The mail endpoints (enterprise/mail and billing/mail) accept arbitrary recipient list, subject, and body from the caller, then dispatch emails via send_inner_email_task with no rate limiting, recipient validation, or content sanitization. An attacker with the inner API key can use these endpoints as an open email relay for phishing or spam, sending arbitrary content to any email address. The substitutions parameter is passed directly to the email template engine, which may allow template injection depending on implementation.

Multiple plugin invoke endpoints catch broad Exception and return str(e) in the response body via BaseBackwardsInvocationResponse(error=str(e)). This can leak internal implementation details such as database connection strings, file paths, model provider error messages, and stack trace fragments to the caller.

The enterprise workspace creation endpoint accepts an owner_email in the request body and assigns that account as workspace owner without verifying that the caller has any relationship with or authority over the specified email address. An attacker with the enterprise inner API key can create workspaces and assign ownership to any existing account, potentially granting the target user unintended access or creating confusion about workspace ownership hierarchies.

The enterprise_inner_api_only and billing_inner_api_only decorators both use the same INNER_API_KEY for authentication, meaning there is no privilege separation between enterprise and billing internal callers. A billing service compromise grants full enterprise workspace management capabilities and vice versa. The key comparison uses simple '!=' which may be vulnerable to timing attacks, though practical exploitation is difficult.
